abstract: Tin-based materials have emerged as highly promising catalysts for the selective electrochemical CO₂ reduction reaction (CO₂RR), yet a fundamental understanding of their catalytic intermediates and critical surface species remains elusive. In this study, we developed a series of structurally well-defined single-Sn-atom catalysts as model systems to probe their electrochemical reactivity. We demonstrate that the activity and selectivity for formic acid production are strongly correlated with axial oxygen coordination at Sn(IV)-N₄ sites (O-Sn-N₄). This configuration achieves a peak Faradaic efficiency of 89.4% for HCOOH with a partial current density of 74.8 mA·cm⁻² at -1.0 V vs. RHE. By integrating *operando* X-ray absorption, attenuated total reflectance surface-enhanced infrared, Raman, and ¹¹⁹Sn Mössbauer spectroscopies, we successfully captured surface-bound bidentate tin carbonate species during the reaction and mapped the evolution of the electronic and coordination environments. Density functional theory (DFT) calculations further reveal that O-Sn-N₄ sites favor the formation of Sn-O-CO₂ species, which optimally tunes the adsorption geometry of reactive intermediates. This pathway significantly lowers the energy barrier for the hydrogenation of *OCHO compared to the *COOH pathway preferred by standard Sn-N₄ sites, thereby dramatically enhancing the conversion of CO₂ to formic acid.
